results.possibleIdentity: Michael Stanley Kirby (1931–1997) was an influential American artist, critic, and scholar who was deeply embedded in the New York avant-garde scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. He is best known for his work with the 'Happenings' movement and his association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Kirby was a participant in E.A.T. performances and discussions, most notably documenting the '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering' event (1966), which was the catalyst for the founding of E.A.T. He also contributed to the collective's work on the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan. As an artist, his practice included performance art, conceptual 'embedded sculptures,' and structuralist film. Academically, he served as a professor of drama at New York University and was the editor of 'The Drama Review' (TDR) from 1969 to 1986. His seminal books include 'Happenings' (1965) and 'The Art of Time: Essays on the Avant-Garde' (1969), where he theorized the intersection of technology, time, and performance.

results.possibleIdentity: Jan Hurwitz was a researcher and administrative staff member for Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a prominent non-profit organization founded in 1966 to foster collaborations between artists and engineers. Active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, she worked closely with E.A.T. co-founder Billy Klüver and researcher Julie Martin. Hurwitz is credited as a researcher and assistant for several major E.A.T. projects, most notably the design and development of the Pepsi Pavilion for the Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan. She also played a significant role in compiling organizational documentation, including the E.A.T. bibliography and archival materials now held in collections such as the Getty Research Institute. While often categorized as a researcher or project assistant within the collaborative framework of E.A.T., her work was integral to the realization of technologically advanced artworks during this period.
